content: Imagine you are managing a shared ledger for a high-throughput trading system where thousands of threads update balances in parallel. The naive approach to reading this data is to grab a global mutex, check the value, and release it; however, this creates a severe serialization bottleneck that kills throughput under load. A better strategy involves tracking changes with a sequence counter instead of holding locks on every access. This pattern is known as Seqlock or RCU-style locking, and its elegance lies in how it decouples read and write operations to maximize concurrency while guaranteeing atomic visibility. The core mechanism relies on two fields: an atomic integer that acts as the sequence number, which increments with each update, and a pointer to the actual data structure containing that same version of the data. When a thread wishes to perform a read operation using this pattern, it first loads the current value of the sequence counter into a local register; if the subsequent load of the data pointer matches the sequence counter it just loaded, then the read is guaranteed to be consistent and ABA-safe because any intervening update would have incremented the counter. The power of this design comes from the specific ordering of memory operations required by the architecture: on most modern CPUs, a read operation must perform a full store fence before proceeding to ensure that writes made by other threads are visible, so the sequence number load acts as an implicit barrier preventing reordering with the data pointer load. This ensures that when we see a particular sequence number N, the corresponding data pointer really reflects what was written at time T where sequence counter went from N-1 to N. The critical insight for why this avoids ABA problems is that if you were using simple memory barriers without tracking the version, another thread could update data from value X to Y back to X while holding a lock or performing writes in between your read and write; with Seqlock, that intermediate state never becomes visible because the sequence counter increments during updates. So when you retry on an odd count—specifically checking if the loaded sequence number matches the data pointer's associated version—you effectively detect any interference from concurrent writers without ever needing to acquire a lock or perform expensive atomic comparisons of pointers alone. In Rust terms, this translates directly into using std::sync::AtomicUsize for the counter alongside a raw pointer to your structure, where you use AtomicUsize::load_relaxed followed by a manual check against the data's version field before returning it; if the check fails because another thread has modified the data since we started reading, we simply retry the entire read sequence until consistency is achieved. This approach provides an excellent balance between performance and correctness for read-heavy workloads where writers are relatively infrequent but must still be protected from visibility issues caused by reordering or concurrent updates.
